pattern: Model each array's total as either a fixed value or an adjustable range once you know how many zeros it holds, then reconcile the two arrays' totals to find the smallest common sum.
hints: A zero can become any positive integer, so an array containing at least one zero doesn't have a fixed sum — it has a minimum achievable sum (replace every zero with 1) and can be pushed arbitrarily higher than that.
An array with no zeros at all has a sum that can never change — treat it as a fixed target that the other array's adjustable sum must hit exactly.

If both arrays contain zeros, the answer is the larger of their two minimum sums (push the smaller side's zeros up further); if only one contains zeros, check whether the other's fixed sum is at least that minimum; if neither contains zeros, the two sums must already be equal.

edge_inputs.description: no zeros anywhere, sums already equal
no zeros anywhere, sums unequal so it's impossible
one side is entirely zeros, the other fixed side is large enough
the fixed side's sum is smaller than the flexible side's minimum achievable sum
minimum-length arrays, both a single zero
no zeros, large mismatched values at the top of the value range
both sides have zeros with very different minimums, requiring the larger minimum to win
many zeros against a single small fixed value that can't cover the minimum

stress.description: large arrays on both sides with a mix of zeros and random positive values

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minSum(self, nums1, nums2):
        sum1, z1 = sum(nums1), nums1.count(0)
        sum2, z2 = sum(nums2), nums2.count(0)
        lo1 = sum1 + z1 if z1 else sum1
        lo2 = sum2 + z2 if z2 else sum2
        lo = min(lo1, lo2)
        hi = max(lo1, lo2) + 5
        for target in range(lo, hi + 1):
            ok1 = (target == sum1) if z1 == 0 else (target >= lo1)
            ok2 = (target == sum2) if z2 == 0 else (target >= lo2)
            if ok1 and ok2:
                return target
        return -1
